items.q: What is the sequence of headland erosion from crack to stump?
items.correct: Crack to cave to arch to stack to stump — waves widen cracks into caves, two caves meet to form an arch, the arch collapses leaving a stack, and the stack erodes to a stump.
items.misconception: Stacks form where rocks fall from the cliff into the sea.
items.random_wrong: Stacks form when the sea level rises and floods a hill.
items.why: It's a sequence of erosion at the same site, not random rockfall or sea level rise. Each landform is the predecessor of the next, with hydraulic action and abrasion driving the progression.

items.q: Why might managed retreat be more sustainable than building a sea wall?
items.correct: Sea walls are expensive, deflect erosion elsewhere and reflect wave energy worsening beach loss; managed retreat works with natural processes, creates habitat and is cheaper long-term.
items.misconception: Sea walls always solve coastal erosion problems permanently.
items.random_wrong: Managed retreat means letting all coastal towns disappear into the sea.
items.why: Sea walls protect one stretch but cause terminal scour and sediment starvation downdrift. Managed retreat selectively allows the coast to adjust — controversial socially but ecologically sound.

items.q: What is the difference between primary and secondary effects of an earthquake?
items.correct: Primary effects occur immediately as a direct result of shaking (building collapse, deaths); secondary effects follow afterwards (fires, tsunamis, disease, homelessness).
items.misconception: Primary effects are the worst effects and secondary effects are minor.
items.random_wrong: Primary effects happen first and secondary effects happen the next day.
items.why: Secondary effects can be far worse — the 2011 Tohoku tsunami killed more than the shaking itself. The distinction is about cause (direct vs cascading), not severity or strict timing.
The answer examiners want is: Relph. “Tuan” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
Correct answer: Ruth Glass. “Doreen Massey” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: In what year was Liverpool European Capital of Culture?
items.why: Exact answer: 2008 AD. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
